nodes.title: Gender & articles: le, la, un, une
nodes.explanation: Here's the one rule that changes everything: French nouns don't come naked. Every noun arrives with its article glued to it — le café, la rue, un film, une idée. You're not learning café, you're learning le café. Do that from the start and gender never becomes a problem to solve later. Now, how do you know which one? The ending of the word gives you a strong hint. If it ends in -tion, -sion, or -té — think situation, solution, liberté — it's feminine, so it's la. Those -tion words you already know from cognate bridges? All la. Words ending in -age, -ment, or -eau tend to be masculine — le message, le moment, le gâteau. Not perfect, but it gets you right most of the time. And when you're wrong? Genuinely no catastrophe — a French person will understand you, maybe not even notice. Now here's the street angle: unlike English, French never drops the article. You can drop ne, you can drop the subject sometimes, but the article stays — it's glued on at any speed. T'as vu la meuf? J'ai pris le métro. The article is always there. So own it, stick it on, and keep going.

canonical_examples.formal: Est-ce que tu veux une table ?

canonical_examples.english: Did you see the girl who speaks French?
canonical_examples.formal: Tu as vu la fille qui parle français ?
canonical_examples.street: T'as vu la fille qui parle français ?

drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu as vu le film ?

drills.french_street: Du coup, la situation c'est grave ?

drills.french_formal: C'est le film que j'ai vu.

drills.english: I have to take the metro.
The girl who speaks French — did you see her?
drills.french_formal: La fille qui parle français, est-ce que tu l'as vue ?
drills.french_street: La fille qui parle français, t'as vu ?

nodes.title: Plurals & adjective agreement
nodes.explanation: Here's something that will save you a lot of worry. In spoken French, the plural -s on a noun is completely silent. You will never hear it. What carries the plural for you is the article — le becomes les, un becomes des. That's it. Les amis, des trucs — the noun itself sounds exactly the same as singular. Train your ear on that article swap and you've got plural. Now adjectives do agree in gender and number in writing, but most of the time you can't hear it either — les petits cafés and le petit café sound identical on the adjective. The audible cases are the ones to lock in: petit sounds like puh-TEE, but petite wakes up that final T — puh-TEET. Same logic with bon and bonne — the N is silent in the masculine, alive in the feminine. Blanc, blanche — the C and the SH appear out of nowhere in the feminine. So the rule of thumb is this: the final consonant of an adjective is sleeping in the masculine and wakes up in the feminine. On the street, plural is almost entirely carried by les versus le, des versus un. Get those article swaps clicking at speed and you sound natural straight away.

canonical_examples.english: We're going to eat some small things.
canonical_examples.formal: Nous allons manger des petites choses.
canonical_examples.street: On va manger des petites choses.

canonical_examples.english: It's not the same, the white table and the white tables.
canonical_examples.formal: Ce n'est pas pareil, la table blanche et les tables blanches.
canonical_examples.street: C'est pas pareil, la table blanche et les tables blanches.

drills.french_formal: Ce n'est pas la même chose.

drills.english: We can eat some good things.
drills.french_formal: Nous pouvons manger des bonnes choses.
drills.french_street: On peut manger des bonnes choses.

drills.english: I want them. (the small ones, feminine)
Did you finish the small coffees?
drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu as fini les petits cafés ?

drills.english: We're going to see some good films.
drills.french_formal: Nous allons voir des bons films.
drills.french_street: On va voir des bons films.

drills.english: It's the good solution, the white one.
drills.french_formal: C'est la bonne solution, la blanche.
drills.french_street: C'est la bonne solution, la blanche.

drills.english: I don't know the small ones.
drills.french_formal: Je ne connais pas les petits.

drills.english: It's the good ones that I want.
drills.french_formal: Ce sont les bonnes que je veux.

drills.english: We have to pay for the white tables.
drills.french_formal: Nous devons payer pour les tables blanches.
drills.french_street: On doit payer pour les tables blanches.

drills.english: So, it was a good small thing.
drills.french_formal: Donc, c'était une bonne petite chose.
drills.french_street: Du coup, c'était une bonne petite chose.

nodes.title: Adjective placement: before or after?
nodes.explanation: Here's the big reversal from English: in French, adjectives almost always go after the noun. So 'a black coffee' becomes un café noir — the colour lands after. 'A difficult question' is une question difficile. Just flip your English instinct and you're already right ninety percent of the time. Now, there's a small VIP club of adjectives that insist on going before the noun — think of them as the highest-frequency words, the ones you'll use every single day: bon, petit, grand, gros, beau, jeune, vieux, nouveau, mauvais. So it's une bonne idée, un petit café, un vieux film. You'll hear these constantly. One thing worth knowing: a handful of these words can shift meaning depending on position — un grand homme means a great man, but un homme grand just means a tall man. Don't stress that for now, just notice it exists. The really fun part is street French — people use the before-club words as intensifiers all the time. C'est un gros problème. T'as une petite question ? C'est une vraie galère. That gros isn't about size, it's about emphasis. Same energy as 'major problem' in English. Once you feel that, you sound instantly more natural.

canonical_examples.english: Do you have a difficult question?
canonical_examples.formal: Est-ce que tu as une question difficile ?

canonical_examples.english: It's a real nightmare, I don't know.
canonical_examples.formal: C'est une vraie galère, je ne sais pas.
canonical_examples.street: C'est une vraie galère, chais pas.

drills.french_formal: Ce n'est pas une mauvaise idée.

drills.english: Do you want a good coffee?
drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu veux un bon café ?

drills.english: We can eat at a good restaurant.
drills.french_formal: Nous pouvons manger dans un bon restaurant.
drills.french_street: On peut manger dans un bon resto.

drills.english: I have to see a difficult film.
drills.french_formal: Je dois voir un film difficile.

drills.english: I'm going to a good restaurant.
drills.french_formal: Je vais dans un bon restaurant.

drills.english: It's going to be a big problem.
drills.french_formal: Ça va être un gros problème.
drills.french_street: Ça va être un gros problème.

drills.english: I ate at a good small restaurant.
drills.french_formal: J'ai mangé dans un bon petit restaurant.
drills.french_street: J'ai mangé dans un bon petit resto.

drills.english: I want it — the good coffee.
drills.french_formal: Je le veux — le bon café.

drills.english: It's a good film that I saw.
drills.french_formal: C'est un bon film que j'ai vu.
drills.french_street: C'est un bon film qu'j'ai vu.

drills.english: Could you give me a small question?
drills.french_formal: Pourriez-vous me donner une petite question ?
drills.french_street: Tu pourrais me donner une petite question ?

drills.english: So, it's a real big problem, I don't know.
drills.french_formal: Du coup, c'est un vrai gros problème, je ne sais pas.
drills.french_street: Du coup, c'est un vrai gros problème, chais pas.

nodes.title: Prepositions: à, de, en, au, chez
nodes.explanation: Here's something you already do in English without thinking — you glue small words onto places and people. In French those small words are prepositions, and the big news is that two of them actually merge with the article. When à meets le, they fuse into au — you can't say à le, it just doesn't exist in French. So it's au café, au bar, au resto. When à meets les, you get aux — aux toilettes, aux États-Unis. Same deal with de: de plus le becomes du — le menu du jour, the dish of the day. De plus les becomes des. Now for places: with feminine countries you use en — en France, en Espagne — and with cities you use à — à Paris, à Lyon. That pattern is very consistent, so trust it. Then there's chez, which is one of those beautifully compact French words. It means at someone's place or their professional space — chez moi is at mine, chez toi is at yours, chez le médecin is at the doctor's. On the street these prepositions don't change, they just glide into the flow — j'suis chez moi, on va au bar, t'as vu le menu du jour ? You already know aller and the futur proche, so you've got the perfect launchpad for all of this.

canonical_examples.english: It's the dish of the day.

canonical_examples.formal: Elle est en France, à Paris.
canonical_examples.street: Elle est en France, à Paris.

canonical_examples.english: We have to go to the doctor's.
canonical_examples.formal: Nous devons aller chez le médecin.
canonical_examples.street: On doit aller chez le médecin.

canonical_examples.english: Did you see the menu of the restaurant?
canonical_examples.formal: Tu as vu le menu du restaurant ?
canonical_examples.street: T'as vu le menu du resto ?

drills.english: I want to eat at yours.
Can you come to my place?
The solution of the day — that's not normal.
drills.french_formal: La solution du jour — ce n'est pas normal.
drills.french_street: La solution du jour — c'est pas normal.

drills.english: We have to go to the toilets.
Did you eat at the restaurant?
So, we're going to the bar.
drills.french_street: Du coup, on va au bar.

drills.english: I was at my friend's place.

drills.french_formal: C'est le café que je veux.

drills.english: I'm going to eat at yours, it's going to be great.
drills.french_formal: Je vais manger chez toi, ça va être super.
drills.french_street: J'vais manger chez toi, ça va être super.

drills.english: I'd like a table at the restaurant.
drills.french_formal: Je voudrais une table au restaurant.

drills.english: I don't know — we can eat at the bar.
drills.french_formal: Je ne sais pas — nous pouvons manger au bar.
drills.french_street: Chais pas — on peut manger au bar.

drills.english: The girl who lives in France is at my place.
drills.french_formal: La fille qui habite en France est chez moi.
drills.french_street: La fille qui habite en France est chez moi.

nodes.title: Question forms: the full toolkit
nodes.explanation: Here's something that's going to unlock the way real French people actually talk. When French speakers ask a question, most of the time they just say a normal sentence and let their voice go up at the end. Tu viens? C'est bon? That rising tone does all the work — no rearranging words, nothing. Now here's the street move that's going to feel immediately natural to you: question words go at the END. Not at the front like in a textbook. Tu fais quoi? Il arrive quand? C'est où? You already know those words and that structure — you're just flipping the question word to the back and riding the intonation up. Est-ce que is your neutral, careful register — perfectly correct, never wrong, just a touch more formal than the street version. And inversion — viens-tu, où vas-tu — you'll hear it, maybe read it, but you never need to produce it. It's like the subjunctive of question forms: recognise it, don't fear it, move on. On the street, these question words — quoi, où, quand, qui, comment, pourquoi, combien — all sit at the end and let the sentence breathe before them. C'est qui lui? Tu pars quand? Ça coûte combien? That's it. That's real French.

canonical_examples.formal: Pourquoi est-ce que tu ne peux pas venir ?

drills.english: Are you going to the restaurant?
drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu vas au restaurant ?

drills.english: So we're going to eat where?
drills.french_formal: Du coup, on va manger où ?
drills.french_street: Du coup, on va manger où ?

drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu veux le voir ?

drills.english: The girl who's speaking — she's leaving when?
drills.french_formal: La fille qui parle, elle part quand ?
drills.french_street: La fille qui parle, elle part quand ?

drills.english: That would be great — how much does it cost?
drills.french_formal: Ce serait super — ça coûte combien ?
drills.french_street: Ça s'rait super — ça coûte combien ?

nodes.title: Beyond pas: jamais, rien, plus, personne
nodes.explanation: You already know how pas works — it sits right after the verb, and in real speech the ne vanishes. Well, jamais, rien, plus, and personne all work the exact same way. Same slot, same ne-drop. Je ne vais jamais là-bas becomes j'vais jamais là-bas. Je n'ai rien dit becomes j'ai rien dit. It's not laziness, it's just French. Jamais is never, rien is nothing, plus is no more or not anymore — and watch out, that s is completely silent: j'en peux plus sounds like j'en peu plu. Personne means nobody, and here's the cool bit — rien and personne can actually flip to the front and become the subject: personne sait, rien se passe. When they're up front the ne can hang around, but on the street it still usually drops. You can even stack them: y'a jamais personne ici — there's never anyone here. That's three negatives in one go and it's completely normal French. So the pattern is locked: whatever you built with pas, swap it out for any of these four and you're done. J'ai rien fait, j'y vais plus, il vient jamais — you're already speaking it.

canonical_examples.formal: Il n'y a jamais personne ici.

drills.french_formal: Je ne peux plus le faire.

drills.english: We never go to the restaurant anymore.
drills.french_formal: Nous n'allons plus jamais au restaurant.
drills.french_street: On va plus jamais au resto.

drills.english: There's never anyone at the beach.
drills.french_formal: Il n'y a jamais personne à la plage.
drills.french_street: Y'a jamais personne à la plage.

drills.french_formal: Il ne se souvient de rien.
C'est comme s'il n'y a plus rien.

drills.english: I was never tired back then.
drills.french_formal: Je n'étais jamais fatigué à cette époque.

nodes.title: Object pronoun order: me le, te la, le lui
nodes.explanation: You already know how to drop a single pronoun in front of a verb — je le veux, je lui parle. Now you're going to stack two of them, and there's a dead-simple rule for the order. When you've got me, te, nous, or vous in the mix, they always go first — so it's tu me le donnes, not tu le me donnes. Think of it as: the person comes before the thing. But watch what happens when you swap to a third-person indirect pronoun like lui or leur — now the thing jumps in front: je le lui dis, je le lui donne. Him or her goes last. And the whole cluster — both pronouns together — sits before the verb, just like a single pronoun does. In the passé composé that means before the auxiliary: il me l'a dit, je te l'ai donné. Now here's the street layer, and this is just about recognition for now. In fast real speech that cluster gets crushed. Tu me le donnes becomes tu m'le donnes, and je te l'ai dit gets squeezed all the way down to j't'l'ai dit. You'll hear it before you produce it, and that's exactly how it should go.

canonical_examples.english: Can you give it to me?
We have to give it to them.

drills.english: Can you give it to me?
I want to give it to him.
You have to give it to me.
I'm going to give it to him.
She already gave it to me.
It's not possible, I told you.
drills.french_formal: Ce n'est pas possible, je te l'ai dit.

drills.english: We have to give it to them.
He was giving it to her.
I saw it — he told me.
drills.french_formal: J'ai vu — il me l'a dit.

drills.english: That's rough, she didn't give it to me.
drills.french_formal: C'est chaud, elle ne me l'a pas donné.
drills.french_street: C'est chaud, elle m'l'a pas donné.

drills.english: I know, I'm going to tell him.
drills.french_formal: Je sais, je vais le lui dire.

nodes.title: y & en: the two little pronouns, deep dive
nodes.explanation: You already know y and en from the v1 list — now let's go deep. Think of y as a little stand-in for à plus a place or thing. You go to the café? J'y vais. You're thinking about the problem? J'y pense. The y just swallows that whole à-phrase. En does the same job but for de — and for quantities. You want some? J'en veux. You want two? J'en veux deux. You have loads? J'en ai plein. The quantity word always comes after the verb, hanging on the end — that's your signal. Now the street angle, and this is big: y'a and y'en a are the existentials you hear all day long. Y'a rien means there's nothing. Y'en a plus means there's none left. Y'en a trois means there are three of them. These aren't fancy grammar — they're the air French speakers breathe. Then there are the frozen expressions you just grab whole: ça y est means that's it, it's done. On y va means let's go. T'en fais pas means don't worry about it. J'en peux plus means I can't take it anymore. Lock those chunks in — they'll carry you through dozens of conversations.

drills.english: There's some left, there are two of them.
drills.french_formal: Il y en a encore, il y en a deux.
drills.french_street: Y'en a encore, y'en a deux.

drills.french_formal: Je ne peux pas y aller aujourd'hui.
Est-ce que tu en as mangé ?

drills.english: There's nothing there, it's not possible.
drills.french_formal: Il n'y a rien, ce n'est pas possible.

drills.english: I want to go there, I've been thinking about it.
drills.french_formal: Je veux y aller, j'y pense.

drills.english: So, are we going? I can't take it anymore.
drills.french_formal: Donc, on y va ? Je n'en peux plus.
drills.french_street: Du coup, on y va ? J'en peux plus.

nodes.title: Comparatives & superlatives: plus, moins, aussi
nodes.explanation: You already know c'est — now you're going to weaponise it. To say something is more, less, or as something as something else, French gives you three little words: plus, moins, aussi. The pattern is dead simple: c'est plus cher qu'à Paris — it's more expensive than in Paris. C'est moins cher — less expensive. C'est aussi bien — just as good. Notice que links the two things being compared, and it contracts to qu' before a vowel, just like in English 'that'. Now, two irregulars you have to own: bon, meaning good, doesn't do plus bon — it becomes meilleur. Bien, meaning well or good as an adverb, becomes mieux. So c'est mieux, c'est meilleur — those two are your bread and butter. For superlatives, just add le, la, or les before plus or moins: le plus simple, la moins chère. One sound thing to lock in: when plus means more, you hear the s — plus intéressant, plus cher. When it means no more, like je mange plus, the s is silent. On the street, comparisons almost always ride on c'est: c'est mieux, c'est moins cher, c'est carrément plus simple. That's your go-to frame.

canonical_examples.english: It's more expensive than in Paris.
This restaurant is better than the other one.
canonical_examples.formal: Ce restaurant est meilleur que l'autre.
canonical_examples.street: Ce resto est meilleur que l'autre.

canonical_examples.formal: C'est carrément la solution la plus simple.
canonical_examples.street: C'est carrément la solution la plus simple.

canonical_examples.english: I don't want any more — it's more interesting over there.
canonical_examples.formal: Je n'en veux plus — c'est plus intéressant là-bas.
canonical_examples.street: J'en veux plus — c'est plus intéressant là-bas.

drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu veux quelque chose de mieux ?
drills.french_street: Tu veux quelque chose de mieux ?

drills.english: We're going to the least expensive restaurant.
drills.french_formal: Nous allons au restaurant le moins cher.
drills.french_street: On va au resto le moins cher.

drills.english: I want to eat somewhere better.
drills.french_formal: Je veux manger dans un endroit meilleur.
drills.french_street: J'veux manger dans un endroit meilleur.

drills.english: Can you find something less expensive?
drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu peux trouver quelque chose de moins cher ?
drills.french_street: Tu peux trouver quelque chose de moins cher ?

drills.english: It's more interesting than I thought.
drills.french_formal: C'est plus intéressant que je pensais.

drills.english: We have to find the best solution.
drills.french_formal: Nous devons trouver la meilleure solution.
drills.french_street: On doit trouver la meilleure solution.

drills.english: It's going to be better, I think.
drills.french_formal: Ça va être mieux, je pense.

drills.english: The film I saw was better than the other one.
drills.french_formal: Le film que j'ai vu était meilleur que l'autre.
drills.french_street: Le film qu'j'ai vu était meilleur que l'autre.

drills.english: It's totally the most important thing.
drills.french_formal: C'est carrément la chose la plus importante.
drills.french_street: C'est carrément la chose la plus importante.

drills.english: You went to a better place than us.
drills.french_formal: Tu es allé dans un endroit meilleur que nous.
drills.french_street: T'es allé dans un endroit meilleur que nous.

drills.english: In the end it was less expensive than expected.
drills.french_formal: En fait, c'était moins cher que prévu.
drills.french_street: En fait, c'était moins cher que prévu.

nodes.title: Imperatives: commands without a subject
nodes.explanation: Here's something you're going to love — commands in French are actually simpler than what you already know, because you drop the subject entirely. You already know 'tu viens' — well, just cut the 'tu' and say 'viens !' That's it, that's your command. Same with 'vous attendez' — drop the subject, say 'attendez !' Now there's one thing to watch with -er verbs in the tu-form: the 's' disappears. So 'tu manges' becomes 'mange !' — no 's'. It feels wrong for a second, then it clicks. Now, when you want to attach a pronoun — like 'me' or 'moi' — it hooks on after the verb with a hyphen: 'donne-moi', 'dis-moi', 'attends-moi'. And for negative commands you already have your best friend 'pas' from day one — 'touche pas', 't'inquiète pas'. On the street the 'ne' is always gone, and you'll even hear 'pas' drop too — just 't'inquiète' on its own. You already know 'vas-y' and 'j'y vais' — well 'vas-y' is a command, and it's everywhere. Add 's'il te plaît' to soften anything, and you sound completely natural.

drills.french_formal: Ne touche pas ça, s'il te plaît.
drills.french_street: Touche pas ça, s'il te plaît.

drills.french_formal: Ne t'inquiète pas, ce n'est pas grave.

drills.english: Eat something, you want to eat, right?
drills.french_formal: Mange quelque chose, tu veux manger, non ?
drills.french_street: Mange quelque chose, tu veux manger, non ?

drills.english: Wait for me, I'm going to pay.
Look at that, it's not possible!
drills.french_formal: Regarde ça, ce n'est pas possible !

drills.english: Don't go to the restaurant without me.
drills.french_formal: Ne va pas au restaurant sans moi.
drills.french_street: Va pas au resto sans moi.

drills.english: Give it to him, it's important.
Let it go, it's not serious.
drills.french_formal: Laisse tomber, ce n'est pas grave.

drills.english: Don't forget, you have to pay.
Watch what I saw, it's totally crazy.
drills.french_formal: Regarde ce que j'ai vu, c'est carrément fou.
drills.french_street: Regarde c'que j'ai vu, c'est carrément fou.

drills.english: Don't stress, we're going to find a solution.
drills.french_formal: Ne stresse pas, nous allons trouver une solution.
drills.french_street: Stresse pas, on va trouver une solution.

nodes.title: Reflexive verbs: the daily-routine engine
nodes.explanation: Here's the big unlock: French has a whole category of verbs where the action bounces back onto you — you do it to yourself. These are reflexive verbs, and they run on a single simple idea: stick me, te, se, nous, or vous right before the verb. So je lève means I lift something, but je me lève means I lift myself — I get up. That's the engine. And in real spoken French, that me collapses almost immediately: je me becomes j'me — so j'me lève, j'me couche, j'me casse. The e of me basically never survives on the street. Now your daily-routine verbs — se lever, se doucher, s'habiller, se réveiller, se coucher — all work exactly this way. Then there's a social set where the action goes between two people: se voir, s'appeler, se retrouver, se parler. On se voit demain means we see each other tomorrow — that reciprocal meaning comes for free. In passé composé, reflexives always take être, just like your movement verbs — je me suis levé, on s'est vus. You already know être in passé composé, so this is just that same machine with a reflexive pronoun clipped in front.

canonical_examples.formal: Je me lève à sept heures.

canonical_examples.english: We're going to meet up at the café.
canonical_examples.formal: On va se retrouver au café.
canonical_examples.street: On va se retrouver au café.

canonical_examples.english: I got up late this morning.
canonical_examples.formal: Je me suis levé tard ce matin.
canonical_examples.street: J'me suis levé tard ce matin.

canonical_examples.english: We saw each other yesterday — it had been a long time.
canonical_examples.formal: On s'est vus hier — ça faisait longtemps.
canonical_examples.street: On s'est vus hier — ça faisait longtemps.

drills.english: I want to go to bed.
We're going to meet up at the restaurant.
drills.french_formal: On va se retrouver au restaurant.
drills.french_street: On va se retrouver au resto.

drills.english: We talk to each other every day.
drills.french_formal: On se parle tous les jours.
drills.french_street: On se parle tous les jours.

drills.english: What are you called? My name is Sarah.
drills.french_formal: Tu t'appelles comment ? Je m'appelle Sarah.

drills.english: I got up at eight this morning.
drills.french_formal: Je me suis levé à huit heures ce matin.
drills.french_street: J'me suis levé à huit heures ce matin.

drills.english: Did you shower? No, not yet.
drills.french_formal: Tu t'es douché ? Non, pas encore.

drills.english: I didn't get up early — it was rough.
drills.french_formal: Je ne me suis pas levé tôt — c'était chaud.
drills.french_street: J'me suis pas levé tôt — c'était chaud.

drills.english: So we're going to meet up at nine?
drills.french_formal: Du coup, on va se retrouver à neuf heures ?
drills.french_street: Du coup, on va se retrouver à neuf heures ?

drills.english: I don't know what time I get up.
drills.french_formal: Je ne sais pas à quelle heure je me lève.
drills.french_street: Chais pas à quelle heure j'me lève.

drills.english: It's the girl I see every day.
drills.french_formal: C'est la fille que je vois tous les jours.
drills.french_street: C'est la fille qu'je vois tous les jours.

drills.english: I was tired — I went to bed early.
drills.french_formal: J'étais fatigué — je me suis couché tôt.
drills.french_street: J'étais fatigué — j'me suis couché tôt.

drills.english: It's not possible — we didn't see each other.
drills.french_formal: Ce n'est pas possible — on ne s'est pas vus.
drills.french_street: C'est pas possible — on s'est pas vus.

nodes.title: Partitives: du, de la, des — 'some'
nodes.explanation: Here's the thing about French — where English happily says 'I want bread' or 'there's no coffee', French always needs its little 'some' word, and that word changes shape depending on the noun. With a masculine noun you get du: du pain, du café. With a feminine noun, de la: de la bière, de la musique. With plurals, des: des frites, des amis. That's it — du, de la, des. Now here's the beautiful shortcut that makes everything easier. The moment you go negative — pas, plus, jamais — all three collapse into one tiny word: de. J'ai pas de pain, y'a plus de café, y'a pas de lait. One rule kills three forms. Same thing after quantities: beaucoup de monde, un peu de temps, trop de trucs — de every time, no exceptions. On the street, this all stays exactly as it is — partitives never drop — but the sounds glue together at speed. Y'a plus d'lait, j'veux du pain, j'prends d'la bière. That d'gluing into the next word is just connected speech doing its thing. You already know j'en veux from y and en — that en is actually replacing the whole partitive chunk. So you've been using this system already without knowing it.

canonical_examples.formal: Je voudrais de l'eau, s'il vous plaît.

canonical_examples.english: There's some coffee and some fries.
canonical_examples.formal: Il y a du café et des frites.
canonical_examples.street: Y'a du café et des frites.

canonical_examples.formal: Il n'y a plus de lait.

canonical_examples.english: There are a lot of people and too many things to do.
canonical_examples.formal: Il y a beaucoup de monde et trop de choses à faire.
canonical_examples.street: Y'a beaucoup d'monde et trop d'trucs à faire.

canonical_examples.english: We're going to have some beer at the café — we haven't got much time though.
canonical_examples.formal: Nous allons prendre de la bière au café — nous n'avons pas beaucoup de temps.
canonical_examples.street: On va prendre d'la bière au café — on a pas beaucoup d'temps.

drills.french_formal: Il n'y a pas de pain.

drills.english: I'm going to have some coffee.

drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu veux des frites ?

drills.english: We want some water at the café.
drills.french_formal: Nous voulons de l'eau au café.

drills.english: I ate some bread and some fries.
drills.french_formal: J'ai mangé du pain et des frites.
drills.french_street: J'ai mangé du pain et des frites.

drills.english: There was a lot of beer — it's not normal.
drills.french_formal: Il y avait beaucoup de bière — ce n'est pas normal.
drills.french_street: Y'avait beaucoup d'bière — c'est pas normal.

drills.english: I don't have any time, so we're going to leave.
drills.french_formal: Je n'ai pas de temps, donc on va partir.
drills.french_street: J'ai pas d'temps, du coup on va partir.

drills.english: Can you get some water? There's no more coffee.
drills.french_formal: Tu peux prendre de l'eau ? Il n'y a plus de café.
drills.french_street: Tu peux prendre d'l'eau ? Y'a plus d'café.

drills.english: I went to the market and there were loads of people — totally.
drills.french_formal: Je suis allé au marché et il y avait beaucoup de monde — absolument.
drills.french_street: J'suis allé au marché et y'avait beaucoup d'monde — carrément.

nodes.title: il faut & impersonal expressions
nodes.explanation: Here's a beautiful piece of French that does a lot of heavy lifting for you. Il faut — it means you have to, one must, we need. The magic is it's impersonal, so you never have to worry about who's doing the action. Il faut partir — you have to leave. Il faut voir ça — you've got to see that. And with a noun: il faut du temps — you need time. Il faut une réservation — you need a reservation. Now here's the street layer, and this is important: in real spoken French, the il just disappears. Nobody says il faut, they say faut. Faut partir. Faut voir. Faut pas faire ça — don't do that. That dropped faut is everywhere. Compare it to tu dois, which you already know — tu dois is more personal, a bit more direct, sometimes a bit accusatory. Faut is softer, more like giving advice naturally. There's a whole family around this idea too: il vaut mieux, which shortens to vaut mieux — better to. Ça prend — it takes, as in time. Il reste — there's still, there's left. These all work the same impersonal way, no subject to manage. Lean on faut and you'll sound natural immediately.

canonical_examples.formal: Il ne faut pas faire ça.

drills.english: You really have to see this film.
drills.french_formal: Il faut vraiment voir ce film.

drills.english: It's better not to say anything.
drills.french_formal: Il vaut mieux ne rien dire.

drills.english: So, you have to go to the restaurant?
drills.french_formal: Donc, il faut aller au restaurant ?
drills.french_street: Du coup, faut aller au resto ?

drills.english: You have to talk to him.
You shouldn't have done that — that's rough.
drills.french_formal: Il ne fallait pas faire ça — c'est difficile.
drills.french_street: Fallait pas faire ça — c'est chaud.

drills.english: It's going to take a long time.
I want to go but you have to book.
drills.french_formal: Je veux y aller mais il faut réserver.
drills.french_street: J'veux y aller mais faut réserver.

drills.english: It's not possible — we need a reservation.
drills.french_formal: Ce n'est pas possible — il faut une réservation.
drills.french_street: C'est pas possible — faut une réservation.

drills.english: Well, it's better to wait — it takes time.
drills.french_formal: Eh bien, il vaut mieux attendre — ça prend du temps.
drills.french_street: Bah, vaut mieux attendre — ça prend du temps.

nodes.title: Time & date expressions
nodes.explanation: Time in French is built on one anchor: il est, meaning it is. Il est trois heures — it is three o'clock. Add et demie for half past, et quart for quarter past, moins le quart for quarter to. On the street, il est often drops to just c'est trois heures or even t'as l'heure — do you have the time? For schedules — trains, appointments — France uses the 24-hour clock: quatorze heures trente, vingt heures. You'll see it on every screen, so recognise it. Days and months never take a capital and never need an article: lundi, en janvier — just like that, bare. Your anchor words are aujourd'hui today, demain tomorrow, hier yesterday, ce matin this morning, ce soir this evening. For appointments, à does all the work: à huit heures, à lundi, and the gorgeous chunk à demain — see you tomorrow. You already know à toute from your greetings chunk — same instinct, time compressed into a word. For ongoing situations, depuis is your friend: j'habite ici depuis deux ans — I've been living here for two years. Notice French uses the present tense where English uses have been. And c'est quand — when is it? — is your go-to for any schedule question on the street.

canonical_examples.formal: Il est trois heures et demie.
canonical_examples.street: Il est trois heures et demie.

canonical_examples.english: The meeting is at two thirty p.m.
canonical_examples.formal: La réunion est à quatorze heures trente.
canonical_examples.street: La réunion est à quatorze heures trente.

canonical_examples.english: I've been living here for two years.
It's quarter to eight — I have to leave.
canonical_examples.formal: Il est huit heures moins le quart — je dois partir.
canonical_examples.street: Il est huit heures moins le quart — j'dois partir.

drills.french_formal: Il est neuf heures et demie.
drills.french_street: Il est neuf heures et demie.

drills.french_formal: Il est sept heures et quart.
drills.french_street: Il est sept heures et quart.

drills.french_formal: Il est six heures moins le quart.
drills.french_street: Il est six heures moins le quart.

drills.english: The train is at twenty hours fifteen.
drills.french_formal: Le train est à vingt heures quinze.
drills.french_street: Le train est à vingt heures quinze.

drills.english: I want to eat this evening.
I have to be there at eight o'clock.
drills.french_formal: Je dois être là à huit heures.
drills.french_street: J'dois être là à huit heures.

drills.english: I'm going to call him this morning.
I've been working here since January.
I've been waiting for two hours.
I got up at seven this morning.
drills.french_formal: Je me suis levé à sept heures ce matin.
drills.french_street: J'me suis levé à sept heures ce matin.

drills.english: It's not possible — the appointment is at fourteen hours.
drills.french_formal: Ce n'est pas possible — le rendez-vous est à quatorze heures.
drills.french_street: C'est pas possible — le rendez-vous est à quatorze heures.

drills.english: So, are we going next week?
drills.french_formal: Donc, nous allons y aller la semaine prochaine ?
drills.french_street: Du coup, on y va la semaine prochaine ?

nodes.title: Pointing & owning: ce, cette, mon, ton, son
nodes.explanation: Here's something you're already half-doing every time you say 'c'est' — now you're going to point at specific things. 'Ce', 'cette', 'ces' mean this, that, these, all in one. The rule is just gender: 'ce' for masculine nouns, 'cette' for feminine, 'ces' for any plural. So 'ce mec' is this guy, 'cette meuf' is this girl, 'ces gens' are these people — you've got your street commentary toolkit right there. One bonus form: before a vowel sound, masculine 'ce' becomes 'cet' — so 'cet après-midi'. And you can slap '-là' on the end for extra pointing force: 'ce truc-là', like 'that thing right there'. Now, possessives — 'mon', 'ton', 'son' — here's the catch that trips everyone: they track the thing owned, not the owner. So 'sa voiture' means his car or her car depending on context, because 'voiture' is feminine. Mon, ton, son for masculine or vowel-starting feminine nouns — and yes, 'mon amie' even though she's a friend, because 'amie' starts with a vowel. Notre, votre, leur stay the same for both genders, so those are easy. Put it all together and you can point at anything and claim anything — which is basically half of street conversation.

canonical_examples.english: I want this thing right there.
This afternoon we're going to the restaurant.
canonical_examples.formal: Cet après-midi, nous allons au restaurant.
canonical_examples.street: Cet après-midi, on va au resto.

canonical_examples.english: His car is not possible — that's rough.
canonical_examples.formal: Sa voiture, ce n'est pas possible — c'est difficile.
canonical_examples.street: Sa voiture, c'est pas possible — c'est chaud.

canonical_examples.english: My friend — she spoke to him.
canonical_examples.formal: Mon amie — elle lui a parlé.
canonical_examples.street: Mon amie — elle lui a parlé.

canonical_examples.english: These people, I don't know them.
canonical_examples.formal: Ces gens, je ne les connais pas.

canonical_examples.english: This table — your coffee is on it.
canonical_examples.formal: Cette table — votre café est dessus.
canonical_examples.street: Cette table-là — ton café, il est dessus.

drills.english: It's not possible — that thing right there.
drills.french_formal: Ce truc-là, ce n'est pas possible.

drills.english: We can eat at this table.
drills.french_formal: Nous pouvons manger à cette table.
drills.french_street: On peut manger à cette table.

drills.english: These people, do you know them?
This afternoon we have to pay.
I went to see this film.
drills.french_formal: Je suis allé voir ce film.

drills.english: That thing right there, I don't want it.
drills.french_formal: Ce truc-là, je ne le veux pas.

drills.english: Their table — it's been a long time.
This guy, I talked to him.
drills.french_formal: Ce mec, je lui ai parlé.

drills.english: My friend, she's going to come this afternoon.
drills.french_formal: Mon amie, elle va venir cet après-midi.
drills.french_street: Mon amie, elle va venir cet après-midi.

drills.english: These people, honestly, that's totally not normal.
drills.french_formal: Ces gens, vraiment, ce n'est absolument pas normal.
drills.french_street: Ces gens, en fait, c'est carrément pas normal.
